title: The Macro Minute:  Is President Trump losing his own trade war?

Happy Monday out there team42. It's your
skipper here, Darius Dell to present our
macro minute for Monday, April 14th,
2025. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead off
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's schemacro question is, is
President Trump losing his own trade
war? Our answers are according to the
White House, the exemption from the 145%
China tariff and 10% global flat rate on
consumer electronics is temporary and
part of a longerterm plan. Though the
move signals President Trump's
willingness to compromise represents
incremental confirmation of our view
that the bond market activated the Trump
put last week. China's consistently firm
tone indicates that it believes that it
has the upper hand in negotiations. At a
minimum, President Xi's historic
consolidation of power means that he
will likely outlast President Trump's
second term. At a maximum, China
understands and can weaponize the fact
that the other side of a trade war is a
capital war and that the bloated US
public sector can ill afford to lose
one. Elsewhere, Wall Street forecasts
are finally starting to reflect the
gravity of the economic headwinds
implied by such extreme market
volatility. While the decline in the US
dollar is supportive of their still
sanguin outlooks, it is unlikely to
fully offset the collection of headwinds
that continue to be underappreciated by
investors broadly. As such, we do not
believe the net impact of tough comps,
historically elevated policy
uncertainty, tariffs, restricting
immigration, and doge budget cuts is
fully reflected in consensus GDP sales
and earnings estimates.
One right tail risk investors can hope
for is more dysfunction in the US
Treasury market because that will speed
up the timing of the QE asset markets
require from the Fed to truncate the
global debt refinancing air pocket we've
been procing since last fall. The 42
macro global liquidity proxy currently
indicates global liquidity is trending
higher, up 5% on a month-over-month
annualized basis and up 10% on a
three-month annualized basis. The
improvement in global investor balance
sheet capacity is consistent with our
message to clients from early March.
Specifically, that the sharp decline in
the US dollar would be initially bearish
for asset markets because it likely
represent capital outflows from
overowned US assets but also eventually
bullish by flattering global liquidity.
While it appears we have entered the
latter more constructive phase of the
global liquidity cycle, key leading
indicators suggesting modest downtrend
in global liquidity over the medium
term. We are not out of the liquidity
woods yet. Transition to our 42 macro
dashboard as always wrap up with a
question from our community. This one's
regarding twin deficits and global
reserve currency. Says, "Hi, Darius. Are
either or both deficits not a side
effect to a nation's currency being the
global reserve currency? I maybe uh
maybe I've misunderstood it or concluded
it wrong in my memory, but I believe
I've heard Lyn Auton explain something
along those lines and about it being
something like a Gordian not trying to
resolve this." Uh what else comes to
mind uh is that when there's a global
liquidity crisis, the US has to provide
the that that that liquidity somehow,
right? Does that contribute to either of
the deficits? I guess the crux of what
I'm wondering is is could the US have
avoided this deficit situation if it
tried all these years? Uh was this not
something that comes along with just
having the global reserve currency? So,
uh, that's a a phenomenal question and I
think one, um, that's a question that,
uh, Steven Moran, which is the White
House, uh, I want to say the chief
economic adviser for the White House,
uh, Council of Economic Advisors. Uh,
that's the, uh, one of the key questions
he asked and tried to answer in his, uh,
p white paper last fall. I forget the
name now. I'm bling on the title. Uh,
the role the US is I I don't even it's
the Hudson Bay Capital U white paper
from last fall that kind of outlined uh,
several of the policy paths that the
president the Trump administration are
pursuing currently. currently. So, uh,
if you didn't know they were going to
try to kitchen sink this thing, uh, you
just weren't paying attention. Uh, but
anyway, that's neither here nor there.
Uh, getting back into this. Uh, so I
don't I'm not of the view that, uh, the
US is, uh, this was an inevitable
situation. Uh, US fiscal policy is
determined by Congress largely. Uh,
obviously we have elements of tariff
policy that is determined by uh, the
president. Um you know obviously we've
had this sort of um you know in order
for the world to grow in a with the US
dollar being the primary reserve
currency and more importantly the the
currency with which global trade and
global finance is priced in clearly you
need to have a surplus of dollars
flowing out into the world uh in order
for us to uh in order for the world to
grow and have more dollars to you know
invest consume and and ultimately
transact in capital and trade markets.
And so that ultimately requires us to
run persistent trade deficits. But I
would argue our persistent trade
deficits were a function of several
things. Uh one of those important things
is the fact that we the people who
living here in the United States of
America have been very reluctant if at
all to uh to administer austerity at
either the household level or at the um
at the federal at the public sector
level. uh you know we've consistently
chosen to have more a lower net national
savings rate a lower household savings
rate and a lower net national savings
rate because of our fiscal policy than
we probably should have which is
creating a significant vulnerability uh
in the treasury market you know yes it
is true that you know being the reserve
currency uh would have you know um you
know creates an incremental incremental
demand for the dollar that would prevent
the dollar from sort of you know I guess
uh revaluing the way it normally would
uh if you know in a kind of a standard
textbook model on trade. You know, if we
continue to have persistent trade
deficits, historically the dollar would
revalue so that we can rebalance our
demand, international demand with our
international exports. And the reality
is we just have not done that. Uh in
part because there's been this
persistent bid for dollars and dollar
denominated assets as a function of the
way the global reserve currency, the
global system has been set up, you know,
back in Brett Woods in 1945. So this is
something that has caused problems for
us, but we've made those problems much
much much much worse than they otherwise
had to have been or certainly would be
at this particular juncture. Maybe they
would have gotten here 20, 50, 30, 20,
30, 40, 50 years from now, but we got
there to this point much sooner in terms
of being very much on the hook to a
global capital um flows as relates to
our bond market being so the loss of
resiliency in the Treasury market now uh
is a function of the the fiscal large S
we've chosen um you know with the 7%
budget deficits GDP our you know our
economic large s that we've chosen we
the US consumer likes living high on the
hog likes you know using credit to
finance you
layers of consumption that they
otherwise wouldn't be able to with their
income alone. And so, uh, these are
choices we made because we've had the
global reserve currency. We've taken,
you know, we've taken advantage of the
fact that we've had the global reserve
currency and now we're being taken
advantage of in, uh, trade negotiations
and and and so on and so forth. And
obviously, um, we saw what happened in
the bond market last week, uh, you know,
forced the Trump put, uh, to activate
the Trump put. So, um, you know, I said
to our clients in our around the horn
this weekend, I think this is the last
week was the most important week since,
uh, Lehman Brothers, uh, in terms of
what it signaled for the potential loss,
the beginning of the end of the US's
exorbitant privilege and our global
reserve currency status. Um, the
exorbitant privilege we've enjoyed as a
function of our reserve currency status.
I one of our um, you know, one of our
top clients emailed me the other uh, in
response to that. He said, "This wasn't
the biggest week since Leman Brothers,
it was the biggest week since Brent
Woods." And I don't know if I disagree
with that. I certainly don't I I'm more
inclined to agree with with him than I
am to disagree with him. So I'll I'll
